title: RDP Session Hijack
briefing: Host $TARGET logged Event ID 4778 (a session was reconnected to a Window Station) for $USER1, but the reconnect came from client $SRC1 — a different source than the one that originally opened the session. An attacker already on the network reconnected to $USER1's live, still-logged-in RDP session (e.g. tscon to another user's session from SYSTEM), taking over an authenticated desktop without any credentials of their own. Recognise the session takeover and its impact.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 100137. Look at Event ID 4778 win.eventdata.accountName and clientAddress. A reconnect from a NEW clientAddress to an existing session = hijack. There is no 4625/logon-failure and no fresh 4624 — the attacker inherited an already-authenticated session.

options: An existing RDP session was hijacked/reconnected by another party
questions.answer: An existing RDP session was hijacked/reconnected by another party
questions.explain: Event 4778 is a session reconnect; from a new client address to $USER1's live session it means someone took over an already-authenticated desktop.
All of the activity lands on $TARGET; the other hosts are unaffected.

questions.prompt: Why is this dangerous even with no failed logins?
options: The attacker inherits $USER1's already-authenticated session and privileges without needing creds
It only affects the lock screen
questions.answer: The attacker inherits $USER1's already-authenticated session and privileges without needing creds
questions.explain: Hijacking a live session bypasses authentication entirely — the attacker operates as $USER1 with whatever that session already holds.

options: T1563.002 Remote Service Session Hijacking: RDP Hijacking
answer: T1563.002 Remote Service Session Hijacking: RDP Hijacking
questions.explain: RDP session hijack (T1563.002) over RDP (T1021.001). No brute force or credential dump is involved.

options: Hunt for what the attacker did inside the hijacked session
Review who has SYSTEM/tscon rights and restrict them
Ignore — reconnects are always the same user
answer: Hunt for what the attacker did inside the hijacked session
Review who has SYSTEM/tscon rights and restrict them
questions.explain: Kill the session, isolate both ends, hunt the in-session activity, and cut off the local-admin/SYSTEM path that allowed the tscon takeover.
